description.summary: A supernatural figure reported in folk traditions worldwide as the cause of sleep paralysis: a malevolent presence that sits, presses, or 'rides' on the chest of a paralyzed sleeper.
description.full: The Night Hag is the cross-cultural figure invoked to explain sleep paralysis: an experience in which a person, awakening during the transition out of REM sleep, finds themselves unable to move while conscious, often with a sense of crushing chest pressure, difficulty breathing, and an overwhelming feeling of malevolent presence. Almost every documented human culture has a name for this experience and a corresponding entity — Old English mære, Norse mara, Newfoundland 'Old Hag', Japanese kanashibari, Chinese 'ghost pressing on body', Arabic ja-thoom, Turkish karabasan, Yoruba ogun oru, Brazilian pisadeira, and many others. Modern sleep medicine identifies the experience as an isolated episode of sleep paralysis, in which the muscle atonia of REM sleep persists into waking consciousness; hypnagogic and hypnopompic hallucinations occur in roughly a fifth of episodes and supply the perceptual content (figures on the chest, shadow people, abductors). Folklorist David J. Hufford's 1982 study of Newfoundland 'Old Hag' tradition, The Terror that Comes in the Night, was foundational in showing that the experience is biologically grounded but culturally elaborated.

etymology.origin: English compound: 'night' + 'hag'; the Old English word for the experience itself was mære/mare, source of 'nightmare'
etymology.meaning: An old or witch-like woman associated with night-time oppression of sleepers
etymology.first_use: English 'night-hag' attested in early modern usage; 'mare' (the older term for the experience) attested in Old English

related_phenomena.name: Nightmare (mare folklore)
related_phenomena.description: The Germanic mara/mære, the etymological root of 'nightmare' and a direct cognate of the night-hag

related_phenomena.name: Incubus
related_phenomena.description: Male sleep-paralysis demon of medieval European tradition

related_phenomena.name: Succubus
related_phenomena.description: Female sleep-paralysis demon of medieval European tradition

related_phenomena.name: Alp
related_phenomena.description: German nightmare-causing entity associated with sleep paralysis

features.description: Most often described as a hag-like female figure crouching on the sleeper's chest
Sensed as a crushing pressure that prevents breathing or movement
Many cultures describe the entity as a black shadow rather than a fully visible figure

physical.morphology: Variable. Common manifestations include an old woman, a witch, a shadow figure, a ghost, an animal pressing on the chest, or a jinn-like being.

abilities.description: Renders the sleeper unable to move or speak while conscious
Produces a sensation of crushing weight on the chest, often interpreted as the entity sitting on the sleeper
Visual and auditory hallucinations of figures, shadows, voices, or footsteps accompanying the paralysis

patterns: Visits sleepers transitioning out of REM sleep
Pins the sleeper to the bed

range.description: Reports of night-hag-type sleep paralysis encounters are documented in nearly every studied human culture.

first_recorded.source: Cross-cultural folklore traditions; Old English and Old Norse references in early medieval Europe

temporal.seasonal_patterns: None known; episodes correlate with sleep deprivation and disrupted sleep schedules rather than seasons.

origins.cultural_roots: Cross-cultural; the Night Hag/mare/old hag complex is one of the most widely documented folklore parallels worldwide.
origins.earliest_accounts: Old English mære and Old Norse mara are attested in early medieval texts; cognate concepts are documented in pre-modern Chinese, Arabic, and other traditions.
origins.folklore_connections: Closely tied to incubus/succubus traditions in medieval Christian Europe and to djinn-pressing traditions in Islamic cultures.

timeline.event: Samuel Johnson defines 'nightmare' in A Dictionary of the English Language as a morbid oppression of the sleeper resembling pressure on the breast
timeline.significance: Codifies the link between the folkloric night-hag and the experience now known as sleep paralysis
timeline.source: Johnson, S. (1755), A Dictionary of the English Language

timeline.event: David J. Hufford publishes The Terror that Comes in the Night, an experiential-source study of the Newfoundland 'Old Hag' tradition
timeline.significance: Foundational academic work showing the cross-cultural night-hag complex is grounded in a real, biologically constrained experience (sleep paralysis)
timeline.source: Hufford, D. J. (1982), The Terror that Comes in the Night, University of Pennsylvania Press

history.modern_era: Sleep paralysis is now a recognised parasomnia, classified by the International Classification of Sleep Disorders. The night-hag tradition is studied at the intersection of folklore, anthropology, and sleep medicine, including its role in shaping modern reports of alien abduction and shadow-people encounters.

documentary.name: Hufford, The Terror that Comes in the Night (1982)
documentary.description: Experiential-source study of Newfoundland 'Old Hag' tradition documenting the cross-cultural pattern of sleep-paralysis experiences.
documentary.analysis: Foundational academic source for the experiential-source hypothesis: that night-hag traditions worldwide encode a real, biologically constrained experience.

documentary.name: Sleep paralysis (parasomnia)
documentary.description: Sleep paralysis is classified as a REM-related parasomnia in the International Classification of Sleep Disorders.
documentary.analysis: Documents the medical reality underlying the night-hag experience.

paranormal.name: Spirit oppression
paranormal.description: Across many cultures the night-hag is interpreted as a witch, ghost, demon, or jinn that physically oppresses the sleeper.

scientific.name: Sleep paralysis (REM atonia intrusion)
scientific.description: The experience corresponds to an isolated episode of sleep paralysis in which REM-sleep muscle atonia persists into waking consciousness, frequently accompanied by hypnopompic hallucinations.

skeptical.name: Cultural elaboration of a biological experience
skeptical.description: Cultures supply the iconography (hag, witch, jinn, alien) for an underlying neurological event; the entity itself is not real, but the experience is.
evidence_cited: Variation of figures across cultures with constant phenomenology

stories: Reports of 'witch riding' in the Southern United States and African-American communities
Accounts of jinn-pressing in North Africa and the Levant
Newfoundland Old Hag summoning rituals (recited backwards)
folklore.beliefs: Most folk traditions identify the Night Hag as a real, external being; many specify protective practices, sleeping positions, or charms to avoid her.

literature: The Terror that Comes in the Night — David J. Hufford (1982)
film_tv: The Nightmare (2015) — documentary by Rodney Ascher on sleep paralysis
other: Henry Fuseli's 1781 painting The Nightmare, an iconic visual depiction of the night-hag motif

researchers.name: David J. Hufford
researchers.affiliation: University of Pennsylvania / Penn State College of Medicine
researchers.contribution: Experiential-source study of Newfoundland 'Old Hag' tradition; foundational analysis of cross-cultural sleep paralysis folklore

organizations.name: American Academy of Sleep Medicine
organizations.focus: Clinical research on parasomnias including sleep paralysis

research.ongoing_studies: Active research continues across sleep medicine, anthropology, and folklore studies on the prevalence, phenomenology, and cultural framing of sleep paralysis.

sources.title: The Terror that Comes in the Night: An Experience-Centered Study of Supernatural Assault Traditions
